entries.author: St. Cyprian of Carthage
entries.title: PRACTICE OF PATIENCE AMID PERSECUTION.
entries.body: What great patience this is that he who is adored in heaven is not yet avenged on earth! Let us think of his patience, beloved brothers and sisters, in our persecutions and sufferings. Let us show forth the full obedience that is inspired by our expectation of his coming. Let us not hasten with the impious and shameless haste of a servant to defend ourselves before the Lord. Let us rather persevere and let us labor, and be watchful with all our heart and steadfast even to total resignation. Let us guard the precepts of the Lord, so that when the day of wrath and vengeance comes,{fn:0} we may not be punished with the impious and sinners but may be honored with the just and those who fear God. {fn:1}

footnotes.text: Cetedoc 0048, 24.475; FC 36:287*. As God is patient with us in our rebelliousness, we are to be patient with others under conditions of trial and harassment, according to the pattern of Jesus under trial.

entries.author: St. Gregory of Nazianzus
entries.title: SILENT BEFORE HIS OPPRESSORS.
entries.body: A lamb, he is silent{fn:0}—yet he is word,{fn:1} proclaimed by the voice of one crying in the wilderness.{fn:2} He is weakened, wounded{fn:3}—yet he cures every disease and every weakness.{fn:4} He is brought up to the tree{fn:5} and nailed to it{fn:6}—yet by the tree of life he restores us.{fn:7} {fn:8}

entries.author: St. Ephrem the Syrian
entries.title: VICTORY THROUGH SILENCE.
entries.body: The Lord became the defender of truth, and came in silence before Pilate, on behalf of truth which had been oppressed.{fn:0} Others gain victory through making defenses, but our Lord gained victory through his silence, because the recompense of his death through divine silence was the victory of true teaching. He spoke in order to teach, but kept silent in the tribunal. He was not silent over that which was exalting us, but he did not struggle against those who were provoking him. The words of his calumniators, like a crown on his head, were a source of redemption. He kept silent so that his silence would make them shout even louder, and so that his crown would be made more beautiful through all this clamor. {fn:1}

entries.author: St. Cyprian of Carthage
entries.title: THE LIMITS OF SILENCE.
entries.body: He is the One who, although he was silent in his passion, will not be silent finally in the day of reckoning. He is our God, even if unrecognized. He is already known among the faithful and all who believe. When he comes manifesting himself in his second coming, he will not be silent. For although he was formerly hidden in humility, he will come manifested in power.{fn:0} {fn:1}

footnotes.text: Cetedoc 0048, 23.462; FC 36:286**. He who was humble in his passion will come in his glory with power.

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: HIS MEEKNESS FULFILLED MESSIANIC PROPHECY.
entries.body: This silence of our Lord Jesus Christ took place more than once. It occurred before the chief priest, and before Herod, and before Pilate himself.{fn:0} So it was not in vain that the prophecy regarding him had preceded: As the lamb before its shearer was dumb, so he opened not his mouth,{fn:1} especially on those occasions when he did not answer his questioners. Usually he replied to questions addressed to him, but in this case he declined to make any reply. The metaphor of the lamb is used to indicate that his silence does not imply guilt but innocence. When he did not open his mouth as he passed through the process of judgment, it was in the character of a lamb that he did so; that is, not as one with an evil conscience who was convicted of his sins, but as one who in his meekness was sacrificed for the sins of others. {fn:2}

footnotes.text: Cetedoc 1278, 116.4.4; NPNF 1 7:426. His silence attests his innocence, in the manner of a sinless sacrificial lamb.

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: WHO CRUCIFIED JESUS?
entries.body: Those who cried out that he should be crucified were the Lord’s real crucifiers, rather than those who simply discharged their service to their chief according to their duty. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: PILATE’S ASSUMPTION OF JESUS’ INNOCENCE.
entries.body: Mark, who studies brevity more than any of the Evangelists, has given a concise indication of Pilate’s desire and of his efforts to save the Lord’s life. For, after giving us the statement, And they cried again, ‘Crucify him’ (which makes it clear that they had cried out before for Barabbas to be released), he has appended these words: Then Pilate continued to say{fn:0} to them, Why, what evil has he done?{fn:1} Thus by one short sentence he has given us an idea of matters which took a long time for their transaction. {fn:2}

footnotes.text: In what follows, Augustine argues that Pilate continued to ask what evil Jesus had done. Time is telescoped into one short phrase.
Cetedoc 0273, 3.13.47.332.18; NPNF 1 6:201*. Mark, who may have been addressing a Roman audience, pointedly indicated that Pilate made repeated efforts to provide a fair trial.

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: THE IRONY OF JESUS’ CONDEMNATION COMPOUNDED.
entries.body: The criminal escaped; Christ was condemned.{fn:0} The one guilty of many crimes received a pardon; he who had remitted the crimes of all who confess was condemned. And yet the cross itself also, if you reflect upon it, was a courtroom. In the middle of it stood the final judge. {fn:1}

footnotes.text: Cetedoc 0278, 31.11.34; FC 88:40*. The one being judged is the final judge.

entries.author: St. Cyprian of Carthage
entries.title: HIS SCOURGING VINDICATED.
entries.body: He himself suffered the lash, in whose name his servants now scourge the devil and his angels.{fn:0} He who now crowns the martyrs with eternal garlands was himself crowned with thorns.{fn:1} {fn:2}

footnotes.text: Cetedoc 0048, 7.132; FC 36:270. Justice is reversed: the whipped becomes the judge, garlands replace thorns.

entries.author: St. Ephrem the Syrian
entries.title: JERUSALEM’S TREATMENT OF JESUS.
entries.body: [The daughter of Zion] repaid him with evil for the immensity of his grace. The Father had washed her from her blood, but she defiled his Son with her spitting.{fn:0} The Father had clothed her with fine linen and purple, but she clothed him with garments of mockery.{fn:1} He had placed a crown of glory on her head, but she plaited a crown of thorns for him.{fn:2} He had nourished her with choicest food{fn:3} and honey, but she gave him gall.{fn:4} He had given her pure wine, but she offered him vinegar in a sponge.{fn:5} The One who had introduced her into cities, she drove out into the desert. The One who had put shoes on her feet, she made hasten barefoot towards Golgotha.{fn:6} The One who had girded her loins with sapphire, she pierced in the side with a lance.{fn:7} When she had outraged the servants [of God] and killed the prophets, she was led into captivity to Babylon, and when the time of her punishment was completed, her return [from captivity] took place. {fn:8}

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: JUDGED WITH THE UNGODLY.
entries.body: For Christ was not separated from the ungodly, but was judged with the ungodly; for it was said about him, He was counted among the wicked.{fn:0} {fn:1}

entries.author: St. Cyprian of Carthage
entries.title: THE REVERSAL IN THE FORM OF MOCKERY.
entries.body: He who now gives true palms to the victors was beaten in the face with hostile palms;{fn:0} he who clothes all others with the garment of immortality was stripped of his earthly garment.{fn:1} {fn:2}

entries.author: St. Cyril of Jerusalem
entries.title: HOW THE MOCKERY ECHOED PROPHECY.
entries.body: When they clothed him in purple,{fn:0} it was in mockery, yet ironically it was a fulfillment of prophecy, for he indeed was a king, so even their parody indirectly served divine revelation. Even though they did it in a spirit of derision, still they did it, and his regal dignity was by that symbolically heralded. So, likewise, though it was with thorns they crowned him, it was still a crown.{fn:1} {fn:2}

entries.author: St. Cyprian of Carthage
entries.title: THE SPITTLE OF HIS REVILERS AND THE SPITTLE BY WHICH HE HEALED.
entries.body: In that very hour of his passion and cross, before they had come to the cruel act of his slaughter and the shedding of his blood, what violent abuses he listened to with patience, and what shameful insults he endured! He was even covered with the spittle of his revilers,{fn:0} when, but a short time before, with his own spittle he had cured the eyes of the blind man.{fn:1} {fn:2}

entries.author: St. Cyprian of Carthage
entries.title: THE PARADOX OF HIS BEING “LED OUT.”
entries.body: He who has given the food of heaven was fed with gall;{fn:0} he who has offered us the cup of salvation was given vinegar to drink.{fn:1} He the innocent, he the just, nay rather, innocence itself and justice itself is counted among criminals,{fn:2} and truth is concealed by false testimonies. He who is to judge is judged and the Word of God, silent, is led to the cross. The elements are disturbed, the earth trembles, night blots out the day,{fn:3} the sun withdraws both its rays{fn:4} and its eyes lest it be forced to gaze upon the crime of the people.{fn:5} Though the stars are confounded at the crucifixion of the Lord, he does not speak, nor is he moved, nor does he proclaim his majesty, even during the suffering itself. He endures all things even to the end with constant perseverance so that in Christ a full and perfect patience may find its realization. {fn:6}

footnotes.text: Those particular Jews who called for his crucifixion.

entries.author: Gospel of Nicodemus
entries.title: WHY SIMON WAS COMPELLED TO CARRY THE CROSS.
entries.body: {fn:0} From the many blows and the weight of the cross he was unable to walk. . . . They gave the cross, then, to Simon of the city of Cyrene, who had also two sons by the name of Alexander and Rufus.{fn:1} They did this not because they pitied Jesus, and wished to lighten his load, but because they ever more eagerly wanted to put him to death speedily. {fn:2}

footnotes.text: An apocryphal Gospel probably redacted in the fifth century. Cf. Quast. 1.116.

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: THE BURDEN SHIFTED.
entries.body: When they went out of the Praetorium, Christ was carrying it: but as they proceeded Simon took it from him and bore it. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homily on the Paralytic Let Down Through the Roof 3

footnotes.text: NPNF 1 9:214; TLG 2062.063, 51.53.56-58. A pilgrim at Passover from distant Africa, later known to the apostles, assisted him in his travail.

entries.author: Anonymous
entries.title: TWICE DAWNED ONE DAY.
entries.body:
Already robed as king, he must sustain

Blows from rough palms.

With spit his face is covered.

A thorn-inwoven crown pierces his head,

While to the tree he is fixed.

Wine drugged with myrrh is drunk,

And gall is mixed with vinegar.

His robe is parted and on it lots are cast.

Each one keeps for himself what he has seized.

In this murky gloom,

God silently outbreathed his soul from fleshly body.

The trembling day took refuge with the sun.

Twice dawned one day. FIVE {fn:0}

footnotes.text: Cetedoc 2036, 5.165; An anonymous poetic attempt to epitomize Tertullian’s Five Books Against Marcion. The date and author are uncertain. ANF 4:164; cf. Mt 27:28-50; Mk 15:17-37; Lk 23:26-46; Jn 19:16-30.

entries.author: St. Cyril of Jerusalem
entries.title: WHY WINE AND MYRRH?
entries.body: What sort of gall did they put in my mouth? They gave him, it says, wine mixed with myrrh.{fn:0} Myrrh is like gall in taste, and very bitter. Is the Lord to be thus repaid by you?{fn:1} Are these the offerings you make to your master, O vine? {fn:2}

entries.author: St. Gregory of Nazianzus
entries.title: THE TASTE OF BITTERNESS.
entries.body: He is given vinegar to drink{fn:0} and gall to eat{fn:1}—and who is he? Why, One who turned water into wine,{fn:2} who took away the taste of bitterness,{fn:3} who is all sweetness and desire.{fn:4} {fn:5}

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: EXCEEDINGLY BITTER.
entries.body: The gall is mentioned with a view to express the bitterness of the potion. And wine mingled with myrrh{fn:0} is remarkable for its bitterness. The fact may also be that gall and myrrh together made the wine exceedingly bitter. Again, when Mark says that he did not receive it, we understand the phrase to denote that he did not receive it so as actually to drink it. He did taste it. {fn:1}

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: THE SEDATIVE REJECTED.
entries.body: Be cautious and receive the words of our adversaries in order to spit them out, not to gulp them down and ingest them. Do in this instance what the Lord did when they offered him a bitter drink; he tasted it, and spat it out.{fn:0} So also should you, taste and spit. {fn:1}

footnotes.text: Cetedoc 0278, 6.11.6; FC 78:139*. As he spurned the supposed comforts of adversaries, so does the church reject the pseudo-comforting words of false teachers.

entries.author: Prudentius
entries.title: SPURNING THE CUP.

entries.author: Eusebius of Caesarea
entries.title: HIS MANNER OF DEATH.
entries.body: What death is more shameful than to be crucified? What death worse than this condemnation is conceivable? Even now he remains a reproach among all who have not yet received faith in him! {fn:0}

footnotes.text: POG 224*; TLG 2018.005, 10.8.55.4-9. Crucifixion is the most shameful reproach conceivable.

entries.author: St. John of Damascus
entries.title: WHAT HAPPENED ON THE CROSS.
entries.body:
By nothing else except the cross of our Lord Jesus Christ has death been brought low:

The sin of our first parent destroyed,

hell plundered,

resurrection bestowed,

the power given us to despise the things of this world,

even death itself,

the road back to the former blessedness made smooth,

the gates of paradise opened,

our nature seated at the right hand of God,

and we made children and heirs of God.

By the cross all these things have been set aright. . . .

It is a seal that the destroyer may not strike us,

a raising up of those who lie fallen,

a support for those who stand,

a staff for the infirm,

a crook for the shepherded,

a guide for the wandering,

a perfecting of the advanced,

salvation for soul and body,

a deflector of all evils,

a cause of all goods,

a destruction of sin,

a plant of resurrection,

and a tree of eternal life. ORTHODOX {fn:0}

footnotes.text: FC 37:350*, spaces added to present in poetic stanzas; TLG 2934.004, 84.21-29, 43-48. By the cross all manner of wrongs have been set right.

entries.author: Apostolic Constitutions
entries.title: WORSHIPING AT SET HOURS.
entries.body: Let your prayers be made at the third hour;{fn:0} for then it was that Pilate gave sentence upon our Lord and savior to have him crucified. . . . Let your prayers be made also at the sixth hour; for at that time he was crucified. . . . We observe also the ninth hour of prayer; for at that time the sun was darkened and the earth shaken with horror, as being not able to look upon those bitter cruelties.{fn:1} {fn:2}

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: THIRD OR SIXTH HOUR?
entries.body: One Evangelist says that the Lord was crucified at the sixth hour,{fn:0} and another at the third hour.{fn:1} Unless we understand it, we are left wondering. When the sixth hour was already beginning, Pilate is said to have sat on the judgment seat. In reality when the Lord was lifted up upon the tree, it was the sixth hour. . . . They had killed him already at the time when they were crying out. The government officials at the sixth hour crucified, the transgressors of the law at the third hour cried out. That which some did with hands at the sixth hour, others did with tongue at the third hour. More guilty are they that with crying out were raging, than they that in obedience were serving. {fn:2}

footnotes.text: Cetedoc 0283, 39.63.5.1; NPNF 1 8:264*. Cf. an alternative interpretation in HOG 3.13, NPNF 1 6:203.

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: HE DIED VOLUNTARILY.
entries.body: He who was able not to die unless he willed it, did die because he willed it. So he made a show of principalities and powers, openly triumphing over them in himself. By his death the one and most real sacrifice was offered up for us. Whatever were the charges by which the principalities and powers held us under bondage, he cleansed, abolished, extinguished.{fn:0} {fn:1}

entries.author: Tertullian
entries.title: KING OF GLORY.
entries.body: He was not hailed as the king of glory by the angels until he had been censured on the cross as King of the Jews.{fn:0} . . . You owe your life to him as a debt for these favors. So try as best you can to be accountable to him in the same way that he became accountable for you. Or, be not crowned with flowers at all if you cannot bear the thorns,{fn:1} because with flowers you cannot be crowned. {fn:2}

entries.author: St. Cyril of Jerusalem
entries.title: THE DIFFERENCE BETWEEN THE TWO THIEVES.
entries.body: Of the robbers crucified with him, it was said: He was reckoned among the wicked.{fn:0} Up to this time both were wicked, but one of them was wicked no longer. For one was wicked to the end, yielding not to salvation, and, though his hands were fastened, he struck blasphemously with his tongue. {fn:1}

footnotes.text: TLG 2110.003, 13.30.1-5; FC 64:24. One was wicked and redeemed, the other wicked and unredeemed.

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: THE COMING JUDGMENT.
entries.body: Amid the courtroom of the cross, one robber who believed was freed, the other who insulted him was condemned.{fn:0} He was then signifying in advance what he would do concerning the living and the dead, putting some on his right and some on his left.{fn:1} The one robber was like to those who would be on the left; the other, to those who would be on the right. He who was being judged was anticipating final judgment. {fn:2}

entries.author: St. Cyril of Jerusalem
entries.title: THE PSALM REMEMBERED.
entries.body: Those who passed by wagged their heads, mocking the crucified, fulfilling the Scripture: When they see me, they shake their heads.{fn:0} {fn:1}

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: WITHOUT COMELINESS.
entries.body: Such he appeared on the cross, such when crowned with thorns did he exhibit himself, disfigured, and without comeliness, as if he had lost his power, as if not the Son of God. Such did he seem to the blind.{fn:0} {fn:1}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: ONE WAS PENITENT.
entries.body: In the case of the robbers, one Evangelist says that the two blasphemed,{fn:0} another that one of them reproved him who was reviling the Lord.{fn:1} Yet in this again there is no contradiction. Both things took place, and at the beginning both the men behaved badly. Afterwards when signs occurred, when the earth shook and the rocks were torn apart, and the sun was darkened, one of them was penitent, became more chastened, recognized the crucified One and acknowledged his kingdom. {fn:2}
entries.citation: The Paralytic Let Down Through the Roof 3

footnotes.text: TLG 2062.063, 51.53.59-54.9; NPNF 1 9:214*. Two blasphemed, one repented.

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: THE SIXTH HOUR RATIFIED THE VERDICT OF THE THIRD HOUR.
entries.body: The Lord was crucified at the third hour by the tongues of the populace,{fn:0} at the sixth hour by the hands of the soldiers.{fn:1} When Pilate took his seat before the tribunal, it was about the sixth hour, or early in the sixth hour. When Jesus was nailed to the tree between two thieves, it was the end of the sixth hour. It was between the sixth and ninth hour that the sun was obscured and the darkness prevailed, as we have it jointly attested on the authority of the three Evangelists Matthew, Mark and Luke.{fn:2} {fn:3}

footnotes.text: NPNF 1 7:428**. The testimony of the Evangelists is not contradictory.

entries.author: Eusebius of Caesarea
entries.title: THEIR UNDERSTANDING WAS DARKENED.
entries.body: Note how clearly was fulfilled the prophecy of our Savior’s passion. It was to be a day in which there shall be no light.{fn:0} From the sixth hour to the ninth hour there was darkness over all the earth.{fn:1} . . . This was also fulfilled figuratively by his priestly persecutors, for among them came darkness, cold and ice, following upon their indignities to the anointed One. Their understanding also was darkened, so that the light of the gospel did not shine in their hearts, and their love to God grew cold. Then in the evening the light of the knowledge of the Christ arose, so that they who sat in darkness and the shadow of death saw a great light.{fn:2} {fn:3}

footnotes.text: Eusebius is quoting from Symmachus commenting on Mt 27:45.

entries.author: St. Ambrose of Milan
entries.title: HIS RECOLLECTION OF PSALM 22.
entries.body: As human he doubts. He experiences amazement. It is not his divinity that doubts, but his human soul. He had no difficulty being amazed because he had taken humanity fully to himself. In taking upon himself a human soul, he also took upon himself the affections of a soul. As God he was not distressed, but as a human he was capable of being distressed. It was not as God he died, but as man. It was in human voice that he cried: My God, My God, why have you forsaken me?{fn:0} As human, therefore, he speaks on the cross, bearing with him our terrors. For amid dangers it is a very human response to think ourself abandoned. As human, therefore, he is distressed, weeps, and is crucified. {fn:1}

footnotes.text: Cetedoc 0150, 2.7.25; NPNF 2 10:230**. In the affections of his soul he felt the full terror of forsakenness and so bore our terrors, yet as God-man he serenely bore our forsakenness.

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: APPROPRIATING THE PSALMIST’S VOICE TO HIMSELF.
entries.body: Out of the voice of the psalmist, which our Lord then transferred to himself, in the voice of this infirmity of ours, he spoke these words: My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?{fn:0} He is doubtless forsaken in the sense that his plea was not directly granted. Jesus appropriated the psalmist’s voice to himself, the voice of human weakness. The benefits of the old covenant had to be refused in order that we might learn to pray and hope for the benefits of the new covenant. Among those goods of the old covenant which belonged to the old Adam there is a special appetite for the prolonging of this temporal life. But this appetite itself is not interminable, for we all know that the day of death will come. Yet all of us, or nearly all, strive to postpone it, even those who believe that their life after death will be a happier one. Such force has the sweet partnership of flesh and soul.{fn:1} {fn:2}

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: IDENTIFICATION WITH OUR INFIRMITIES.
entries.body: In his most compassionate humanity and through his servant form we may now learn what is to be despised in this life and what is to be hoped for in eternity.{fn:0} In that very passion in which his proud enemies seemed most triumphant, he took on the speech of our infirmity, in which our sinful nature was crucified with him{fn:1} that the body of sin might be destroyed, and said: My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?{fn:2} . . . Thus the Psalm begins, which was sung so long ago, in prophecy of his passion and the revelation of the grace which he brought to raise up his faithful and set them free. {fn:3}

footnotes.text: Augustine is asking how this messianic psalm anticipated his full identification with our death-resisting human infirmity.

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: PSALM 69 RECALLED.
entries.body: Among the other things prophesied about him, it was also written, They gave me poison for food, and for my thirst they gave me vinegar to drink.{fn:0} We know in the gospel how these things happened. First, they gave him gall. He took it, tasted it, and spit it out. Later while hanging on the cross, that all prophecies might be fulfilled, he said, I thirst.{fn:1} They took a sponge full of vinegar, fastened it on a reed, and offered it to him as he hung there. He took it and said, It is finished.{fn:2} What does It is finished mean? All that had been prophesied before my passion has been fulfilled. What then is there still for me to do? {fn:3}

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: A SIGN OF POWER, NOT NECESSITY.
entries.body: Those robbers crucified next to him, did they breathe their last when they wanted to?{fn:0} They were held fast by the chains of the flesh because they were not the creators of the flesh. Fastened by nails, they were tormented for a long time because they were not masters of their infirmity.{fn:1} But the Lord took on flesh in the virgin’s womb when he wished it. He came forth to humanity when he wished it. He lived in history as long as he wished it. He departed from the flesh when he wished it. This is a sign of power, not of necessity. {fn:2}

footnotes.text: The issue is, did Jesus breathe his last out of necessity or voluntarily?

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: HIS POWER OF DYING.
entries.body: He departed by his [own] power; for he had not come by necessity. And so some marveled more at his power of dying than at his power of performing miracles. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Ephrem the Syrian
entries.title: ALTERNATIVE INTERPRETATIONS.
entries.body: The curtain was torn. [This was] to show that [the Lord] had taken the kingdom away from them and had given it to others who would bear fruit.{fn:0} An alternative interpretation is: By the analogy of the torn curtain, the temple would be destroyed because his Spirit had gone away from it. Since the high priest had wrongfully torn his robe, the Spirit tore the curtain to proclaim the audacity of the pride [of the Jews], by means of an action on the level of created beings. Because [the high priest] had torn his priesthood and had cast it from him, [the Spirit] also split the curtain apart.{fn:1} Or [alternatively], just as the temple in which Judas had thrown down the gold{fn:2} was dissolved and rejected, so too [the Lord] pulled down and rent asunder the curtain of the door through which [Judas] had entered. Or, [it was] because they had stripped him of his garments that he rent the curtain in two. For the heart of the rock was burst asunder,{fn:3} but their own hearts did not repent. {fn:4}

entries.author: St. Gregory of Nazianzus
entries.title: HIS DEATH AS AN UNVEILING.
entries.body: He surrenders his life, yet he has power to take it again.{fn:0} Yes, the veil is torn, for things of heaven are being revealed, rocks split, and dead men have an earlier awakening.{fn:1} {fn:2}

entries.author: St. Gregory of Nazianzus
entries.title: A FEW DROPS OF BLOOD RENEW THE WHOLE WORLD.
entries.body: Many indeed are the wondrous happenings of that time: God hanging from a cross, the sun made dark and again flaming out; for it was fitting that creation should mourn with its creator. The temple veil rent, blood and water flowing from his side: the one as from a man, the other as from what was above man; the earth shaken, the rocks shattered because of the rock; the dead risen to bear witness to the final and universal resurrection of the dead. The happenings at the sepulcher and after the sepulcher, who can fittingly recount them? Yet no one of them can be compared to the miracle of my salvation. A few drops of blood renew the whole world, and do for all men what the rennet does for the milk: joining us and binding us together. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: WHAT WE BEHOLD ON THE CROSS.
entries.body: As they were looking on,{fn:0} so we too gaze on his wounds as he hangs. We see his blood as he dies. We see the price offered by the redeemer, touch the scars of his resurrection. He bows his head, as if to kiss you. His heart is made bare open, as it were, in love to you. His arms are extended that he may embrace you. His whole body is displayed for your redemption. Ponder how great these things are. Let all this be rightly weighed in your mind: as he was once fixed to the cross in every part of his body for you, so he may now be fixed in every part of your soul.{fn:1} {fn:2}

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: WHERE THE WOMEN WERE STANDING.
entries.body: How can we understand the same Mary Magdalene both to have stood afar off along with other women as the accounts of Matthew and Mark bear,{fn:0} and to have been by the cross, as John tells us?{fn:1} It could have been the case that these women were at such a distance as made it quite natural to say at once that they were near because they were at hand there in the sight of him, and yet afar off in comparison with the crowd of people who were standing round about in closer vicinity along with the centurion and the soldiers. It is open for us, then, to suppose that those women who were present at the scene along with the Lord’s mother, after he commended her to the disciple,{fn:2} began then to retire with the view of extricating themselves from the dense mass of people, and from a greater distance looking on at what remained to be done. {fn:3}

footnotes.text: Cetedoc 0273, 3.21.58.348.4; NPNF 1 6:207-8. As they withdrew, the women who were once near may have then viewed the horrible scene from farther away.

entries.author: St. Bede the Venerable
entries.title: WHICH JAMES?
entries.body: How could it be said that the brother of the Lord was not the apostle, but a third James, since Paul also gives him the name of an apostle, saying, I saw none of the other apostles except James, the brother of the Lord;{fn:0} and the evangelist Mark names the same man, not a third, but one of the two Jameses, saying, There were women also watching from a distance, among whom were Mary Magdalene and Mary the mother of the James the younger and of Joseph and Salome?{fn:1} Now greater and lesser are customarily used to establish a difference not among three, but between two. Hence the lesser James is named of Alphaeus, in distinction from the greater, who was the son of Zebedee. {fn:2}

footnotes.text: Cetedoc 1357, 1.139; CAA 15*. There were not three but two Jameses, the younger of whom is James, the son of Alphaeus, whose mother stood afar with the women. Cf. Mt 10:3; 27:56; Mk 3:18; Lk 6:15; Acts 1:13.

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: RECEIVING THE SUPPORT OF WOMEN.
entries.body: For what reason then was he being supported by women?{fn:0} For women, it is said, followed him and ministered to him.{fn:1} It was to teach us from the first that he is ready to receive those who do the good. Could not Paul, who supported others by his own hands, have maintained himself without assistance from others? But you see him receiving and requesting aid. Now hear the reason for it. Not because I want a gift, he says, but I want fruit that may abound to your account.{fn:2} {fn:3}

footnotes.text: Why did Jesus accept substantial support from those least able to offer support?

entries.author: St. Gregory of Nazianzus
entries.title: YESTERDAY HE WAS SLAIN.
entries.body: Yesterday the lamb was slain, and the door posts sprinkled with his blood, while Egypt mourned for her firstborn. But the destroying angel and his sacrificial knife, fearful and terrifying, passed over us,{fn:0} for we were protected by the precious blood. This day we have wholly departed from Egypt, and from Pharaoh, its cruel tyrant, and his oppressive overseers. We are freed from laboring with bricks and straw,{fn:1} and no one forbids us celebrate the festival of our passing over, our pasch, and to celebrate not with the leaven of malice and wickedness but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth.{fn:2} . . . Yesterday I was crucified with Christ; today I am glorified with him. Yesterday I died with him; today I am given life with him. Yesterday I was buried with him; today I rise again with him. {fn:3}
entries.citation: On the Holy Pasch and his Own Reluctance

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: REVIEWING THE CHRONOLOGY OF THE THREE DAYS.
entries.body: Scripture again witnesses that the space of those three days did not imply whole days in their entirety. Rather the first day is counted as whole from its last part, and the third day is itself also counted as a whole from its first part; but the intervening day, i.e., the second day, was absolutely whole with its twenty-four hours, twelve of the day and twelve of the night. For he was crucified first by the voices of the Jews in the third hour, when it was the sixth day of the week. Then he hung on the cross itself at the sixth hour, and yielded up his spirit at the ninth hour.{fn:0} {fn:1}

entries.author: St. Ignatius of Antioch
entries.title: THE SEQUENCE OF EVENTS.
entries.body: At the sixth hour he was crucified. At the ninth hour he yielded up his spirit. Before sunset he was buried. During the sabbath he continued under the earth in the tomb in which Joseph of Arimathea had laid him.{fn:0} {fn:1}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: THE BOLDNESS OF JOSEPH.
entries.body: This was Joseph, who had been concealing his discipleship. Now he became very bold, after the death of Christ. For neither was he an obscure person nor unnoticed. He was one of the council, and highly distinguished, and as we see, courageous.{fn:0} For he exposed himself to death, taking upon himself the enmity of all by his affection to Jesus. He begged for the body and did not desist until he obtained it. Not only that, but by laying it in his own new tomb, he actively demonstrated his love and courage.{fn:1} THE GOSPEL OF ST. {fn:2}

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: WHETHER WE CAN BE SURE THAT JESUS DIED.
entries.body: As the Gospel declares, those who were present particularly marveled at this: After the lament in which he expressed the figure of sin, he immediately gave up his spirit. For those who were suspended on the cross were tortured by a lingering death. Consequently, the legs of the thieves were broken, in order that they might quickly die and be taken down from the cross before the Sabbath.{fn:0} But that he was found to be already dead was a cause for amazement. And we read that Pilate also wondered at this, when the body of the Lord was asked of him for burial.{fn:1} {fn:2}

entries.author: St. Bede the Venerable
entries.title: BURIED WITH UTTER SIMPLICITY.
entries.body: The vanity of the rich, who even in their graves cannot do without their riches, receives its condemnation from the simple and unassuming interment of the Lord. Hence indeed the custom of the church was derived, that the sacrifice of the altar should not be commemorated by wrapping the elements in silk, or any colored cloth, but in linen; as the body of the Lord was buried in clean fine linen.{fn:0} {fn:1}
entries.citation: Exposition on the Gospel of Mark 4.15.46

footnotes.text: Cetedoc 1355, 4.15.1668; CCL 120:638; GMI 434; cf. HOG, loc. cit.

entries.author: St. Athanasius of Alexandria
entries.title: WHETHER HIS BODY REMAINED IN THE TOMB WHEN HE DESCENDED INTO THE NETHER WORLD.
entries.body: This above all shows the foolishness of those who say that the Word was changed into bones and flesh. For if this had been so, there would have been no need of a tomb. For the body would have gone by itself to preach to the spirits in Hades. But as it was, he himself went to preach, while the body that Joseph wrapped in a linen cloth laid away at Golgotha.{fn:0} And so it is demonstrated to all that the body was not the Word, but body of the Word. {fn:1}

footnotes.text: TLG 2035.110, 6.1-6; NPNF 2 4:572. It was not the Word laid in the tomb but the body of the Word. The Word was not changed into bones and flesh but assumed bones and flesh. Jesus descended into the nether world while his body remained in the tomb.
